name: remote-forward
context: Expose a local service through a remote host with ssh -R for reverse tunnels and callbacks
info: Remote forwarding makes the remote side listen on a TCP port and send traffic back over SSH toward a client-side destination. Agents use it to expose a local development server through a reachable bastion, receive webhook callbacks into a private environment, or temporarily publish a tool running on the client machine without opening inbound firewall access.
triggers: forward my local port to a remote host
body:
# Remote Forwarding

A remote forward tells the SSH server side to listen on a port and route connections back through the tunnel to a destination host and port as seen from the client side.

```bash
ssh -R 8080:127.0.0.1:3000 ops@bastion.example.com -N
```

This is useful when the remote side is publicly reachable but the local service is not.

Important operational detail: whether the remote listening socket can bind beyond loopback depends on the SSH server's `GatewayPorts` policy. If you only need the remote host itself to reach the forwarded service, the default loopback bind is usually desirable.

sections.title: Expose a local service remotely
sections.body: Use `ssh -R remote_port:127.0.0.1:local_port host -N` to publish a local process through the remote host. Typical uses include webhook callbacks into a dev machine, temporary demos, or reverse access when inbound connectivity to the client is blocked.

sections.title: Remote bind addresses and GatewayPorts
sections.body: By default, remote TCP listeners usually bind to loopback only. A remote bind address like `0.0.0.0` may require server-side `GatewayPorts` support before the bind succeeds. If the remote side only needs host-local reachability, keep the default or bind explicitly to `127.0.0.1`.
